edges.description: Severe UNC13A loss disrupts central neurotransmitter release during early brain development, impairing acquisition of developmental milestones and structural CNS maturation.
edges.target: Impaired CNS Synaptic Development and Network Maturation

edges.description: Cortical hyperexcitability from impaired UNC13A-dependent synaptic transmission produces early-onset epileptic encephalopathy.
The same cortical hyperexcitability is captured electrophysiologically as slow background activity with multifocal sharp waves and periodic discharges.
Impaired CNS synaptic maturation prevents acquisition of expressive language milestones.
edges.source: Impaired CNS Synaptic Development and Network Maturation

edges.description: Severe impairment of early synaptic network function produces profound developmental disability.
edges.source: Impaired CNS Synaptic Development and Network Maturation

edges.description: Early disruption of UNC13A-dependent brain synaptic development is associated with reduced head growth.
edges.source: Impaired CNS Synaptic Development and Network Maturation

edges.description: Severe early CNS synaptic dysfunction is associated with impaired corpus-callosum development or maintenance.
edges.source: Impaired CNS Synaptic Development and Network Maturation

edges.description: Presynaptic neuromuscular transmission failure weakens neonatal muscle activation and produces profound hypotonia from birth.
Neuromuscular-junction transmission failure can involve ocular levator muscles, producing variable eyelid ptosis.
Severe neonatal neuromuscular weakness compromises feeding and secretion clearance, requiring tube feeding.
intermediate_mechanisms: Neonatal hypotonia and impaired bulbar/respiratory muscle function.

edges.description: Severe presynaptic neuromuscular weakness impairs respiratory muscle function and can progress to fatal respiratory failure.
Impaired motor-terminal neurotransmitter release reduces reflex muscle activation, producing hyporeflexia.
Severe weakness and reduced movement predispose to proximal and knee flexion contractures.

nodes.description: Complete absence of speech development, reflecting profound neurodevelopmental impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: she was unable to sit up and babbled but could not speak

nodes.description: Loss of Munc13-1 function disrupts the balance of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmission in the cerebral cortex, leading to cortical hyperexcitability and seizures. The mechanism involves impaired syntaxin 1B function, as Munc13-1 normally opens syntaxin 1B for SNARE complex assembly.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: Loss of Munc13-1 function predicts that syntaxin 1B is consigned to a nonfunctional closed state; this inhibits cholinergic transmission at the neuromuscular junction and glutamatergic transmission in the brain.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: Inactivation of syntaxin 1B likely accounts for the patient's cortical hyperexcitability because mutations of syntaxin 1B cause febrile seizures with or without epilepsy, haploinsufficiency of the STX1B is associated with myoclonic astatic epilepsy

nodes.description: Markedly abnormal EEG with slow background activity, multifocal sharp waves, and periodic discharges.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: The EEG showed 2-4 Hz background activity over the posterior head regions, nearly continuous multifocal sharp waves in the central regions

nodes.description: Early-onset seizures with cortical hyperexcitability. EEG shows multifocal sharp waves and periodic discharges in one reported patient and burst-suppression correlating with infantile spasms in another.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic UNC13A variants cause a neurodevelopmental syndrome by impairing synaptic function.
evidence.snippet: The syndrome presents with variable degrees of developmental delay and intellectual disability, seizures of different types, tremor and dyskinetic movements and, in some cases, death in early childhood.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: The EEG showed 2-4 Hz background activity over the posterior head regions, nearly continuous multifocal sharp waves in the central regions

evidence.reference_title: Homozygous UNC13A Variant in an Infant With Congenital Encephalopathy and Severe Neuromuscular Phenotype: A Case Report With Detailed Central Nervous System Neuropathologic Findings | Cureus
evidence.snippet: Electroencephalogram (EEG) revealed a burst suppression pattern, which correlated with infantile spasms.

nodes.description: Severe feeding difficulties requiring gastrostomy tube placement, related to both central hypotonia and neuromuscular junction dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: Nissen fundoplasty was performed, and she was discharged home with a gastric tube

nodes.description: Flexion contractures of proximal joints and knees, likely secondary to severe hypotonia and immobility.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: flexion contractures of the proximal joints and knees

nodes.description: Diminished deep tendon reflexes consistent with neuromuscular junction dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: she was markedly hypotonic, hyporeflexic, and could barely move

nodes.description: UNC13A loss reduces synaptic strength throughout early CNS development, disrupting activity-dependent neural-network maturation. In the congenital biallelic loss-of-function presentation, this produces profound global neurodevelopmental impairment with absent speech and limited milestones, and is accompanied by impaired brain growth and corpus-callosum thinning.
nodes.id: Impaired CNS Synaptic Development and Network Maturation

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic UNC13A variants cause a neurodevelopmental syndrome by impairing synaptic function.
evidence.snippet: The UNC13A gene encodes a presynaptic protein that is crucial for setting the strength and dynamics of information transfer between neurons.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: she was unable to sit up and babbled but could not speak

nodes.description: Loss of Munc13-1 at motor nerve terminals severely compromises neuromuscular junction transmission, causing a presynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome. The defect resembles Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome with markedly reduced compound muscle action potentials that facilitate with rapid stimulation.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: EMG studies revealed abnormally low-amplitude compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) at rest

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in UNC13A abolish Munc13-1 protein function, preventing synaptic vesicle priming at presynaptic terminals. This severely depletes the readily releasable pool of synaptic vesicles, reducing neurotransmitter release at both central glutamatergic synapses and peripheral cholinergic neuromuscular junctions.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic UNC13A variants cause a neurodevelopmental syndrome by impairing synaptic function.
evidence.snippet: The UNC13A gene encodes a presynaptic protein that is crucial for setting the strength and dynamics of information transfer between neurons.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic UNC13A variants cause a neurodevelopmental syndrome by impairing synaptic function.
evidence.snippet: Using assays with expression of UNC13A variants in mouse hippocampal neurons and in Caenorhabditis elegans, we identify three mechanisms of pathogenicity, including reduction in synaptic strength caused by reduced UNC13A protein expression, increased neurotransmission caused by UNC13A gain-of-function and impaired regulation of neurotransmission by second messenger signalling.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: Neuromuscular transmission was severely compromised by marked depletion of the readily releasable pool of quanta, but the probability of quantal release was normal.

nodes.description: Head circumference below the fifth percentile, with thin corpus callosum on MRI, reflecting impaired brain development.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: The head circumference was below the fifth percentile

nodes.description: Profound hypotonia present from birth, with inability to feed or clear secretions, requiring ventilatory support.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: She was hypotonic at birth, could not feed or clear her secretions, needed ventilatory support, and required hospitalization for 11 weeks.

nodes.description: Severe to profound intellectual disability with minimal developmental milestones achieved.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: she was unable to sit up and babbled but could not speak

nodes.description: Variable eyelid ptosis reflecting impaired neuromuscular transmission at the levator palpebrae.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: She also had variable eyelid ptosis, no voluntary or tracking eye movements

nodes.description: Progressive respiratory insufficiency leading to ventilator dependence, a major cause of mortality in the severe congenital form.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: she died of respiratory failure

nodes.description: Thinning of the corpus callosum visible on brain MRI, suggesting impaired axonal development.

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: MRI examination of the head showed a thin corpus callosum

evidence.reference_title: Loss of MUNC13-1 function causes microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, and fatal myasthenia.
evidence.snippet: Exome sequencing identified a homozygous nonsense mutation in the N-terminal domain of MUNC13-1 (UNC13A) truncating the protein after 101 residues.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic UNC13A variants cause a neurodevelopmental syndrome by impairing synaptic function.
evidence.snippet: Based on a strong genotype-phenotype-functional correlation, we classify three UNC13A syndrome subtypes (types A-C).

evidence.reference_title: Homozygous UNC13A Variant in an Infant With Congenital Encephalopathy and Severe Neuromuscular Phenotype: A Case Report With Detailed Central Nervous System Neuropathologic Findings.
evidence.snippet: We report the detailed clinical history and central nervous system neuropathologic findings in an infantile case with homozygous UNC13A loss of function variant

nodes.description: Homozygous frameshift variant causing loss of function. Associated with congenital encephalopathy, severe neuromuscular phenotype, infantile spasms with burst-suppression EEG pattern, and death in infancy.

Homozygous nonsense mutation in the N-terminal domain truncating the protein after 101 of 1,703 residues. Associated with the severe phenotype including microcephaly, cortical hyperexcitability, fatal myasthenia, and death at 50 months.
